lens.included_activities: Recurring outsourced vacancy intake, candidate sourcing, resume review, matching, screening, interviewing, referral, placement coordination, and client/candidate communication for external employers. The lens emphasizes established lower-middle-market agencies with repeat employer relationships and approximately $1 million to $10 million of normalized EBITDA.
lens.excluded_activities: Executive search services classified in 561312, temporary help services in 561320, professional employer organizations in 561330, captive internal talent-acquisition teams, pure recruiting-software or job-board businesses, shell or non-operating entities, non-control situations, and registries or owner-dependent practices that cannot support a transferable operating company.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Employers are the principal economic customers. Revenue is generally earned through successful-placement, contingent, fixed, retained-project, or other recruiting-service fees; repeat hiring clients may generate multiple assignments even when each placement is episodic. Candidates are service participants rather than employees of the placement agency under the NAICS definition.

a.bridge: O*NET's national Human Resources Specialists task list is used as a task-map proxy for recruiters at 561311 agencies, while LinkedIn's survey of recruiting professionals supplies an adoption-era productivity cross-check. The estimate maps sourcing, application review, matching, status communication, scheduling, record preparation, and first-pass content work to automatable labor, while reserving interviews, persuasion, nuanced assessment, negotiation, compliance judgment, and relationship management for humans. O*NET includes in-house HR generalists and LinkedIn spans corporate recruiting, search, and staffing, so the proxy may overstate administrative breadth but understate the document and outreach intensity of agency recruiters.
a.rationale: Recruiting contains a large body of digitally mediated search, comparison, drafting, summarization, outreach, scheduling, and workflow-administration tasks that current generative and predictive systems can assist. The work also requires high-contact judgment: O*NET reports daily email and telephone use, near-universal frequent interpersonal contact, application matching, candidate interviews, recommendations, and regulatory knowledge. LinkedIn reports that recruiting professionals already using generative AI save about one-fifth of their workweek, supporting meaningful but incomplete automatable labor content.
a.change_evidence: A time-and-motion study of 561311 recruiters by workflow, employee level, and wage cost, paired with controlled before/after AI deployment results, could materially move the range.
caveats: The task map is occupational rather than industry-specific and includes broader HR work.
Reported time savings among current AI users are not the same quantity as fully automatable wage-weighted labor share.
Specialty, candidate seniority, client process, and compliance requirements cause substantial agency-level variation.

rho.bridge: LinkedIn's September 2024 survey of 1,271 recruiting professionals across 23 countries, including 252 search and staffing professionals, is translated into a five-year realization fraction for a U.S. 561311 operator. Its 37% already experimenting with or integrating generative AI establishes a current adoption foothold, while reported 20% workload savings among users indicates practical workflow impact. The sample's corporate and international mix may overstate well-resourced adoption relative to smaller U.S. agencies, while rapid vendor integration into applicant-tracking workflows could make it understate adoption over five years.
rho.rationale: Implementation should be material because the automatable tasks are already digital and adjacent to applicant-tracking systems, databases, email, calendars, and document workflows. Adoption is nevertheless limited by fragmented data, integration effort, candidate privacy, client-specific processes, bias and adverse-impact controls, quality assurance, and the need for human accountability in recommendations and communications.
rho.change_evidence: Industry-specific longitudinal data on paid AI deployment, workflow penetration, error rates, recruiter utilization, and realized labor hours at independent placement agencies would materially tighten the estimate.
caveats: Survey experimentation can be shallow and need not become sustained production use.
International and corporate respondents may not represent U.S. lower-middle-market agencies.
Employment-law and disability-accommodation controls may slow autonomous screening more than assistive drafting or scheduling.

e.rationale: The NAICS activity is inherently outsourced to employers, and repeat-client agencies can fit the recurring-service lens even though revenue is often tied to individual placements. The eligible share is reduced by very small or owner-dependent recruiters, episodic books of business, registries and casting-oriented firms, weak customer concentration profiles, and businesses below the normalized EBITDA range. Executive search, temporary help, and PEO models are excluded by classification rather than treated as eligible variants.
e.change_evidence: A census of 561311 firms by normalized EBITDA, revenue model, repeat-client share, customer concentration, owner involvement, and control availability would materially change this estimate.
caveats: No public source directly measures the share of establishments satisfying every default-lens criterion.
Establishment counts and enterprise counts differ, particularly for multi-office agencies.
A successful-placement model can produce repeat customers without contractually recurring revenue.

s5.bridge: BizBuySell's 2025 closed-transaction statistics for the broad U.S. service sector are used as a transfer-market baseline and then discounted for the probability that a qualifying 561311 owner sells or recapitalizes within five years. Service transactions increased 4% in 2025 and had a $340,000 median sale price and 2.52 median cash-flow multiple, showing an active small-business transfer channel, but the median business is far smaller than the EBITDA lens. The source therefore likely overstates transaction frequency for the full small-business population while understating the institutional process, buyer depth, and valuation support for larger qualifying agencies.
s5.rationale: Placement agencies are generally asset-light and can be transferred when client relationships, recruiter teams, candidate data, processes, and leadership are not concentrated in the owner. Five-year availability is constrained by the narrow size lens, owner timing, relationship portability, customer concentration, cyclicality, and the difference between an operating business and a recruiter practice built around one individual's network.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year transaction cohort for U.S. 561311 companies in the target EBITDA range, including withdrawn processes, recapitalizations, add-ons, and founder-retention terms, would materially improve the estimate.
caveats: The market source is not NAICS-specific and is concentrated below the target EBITDA range.
Listing and closing data may omit private processes and sponsor-to-sponsor transactions.
Observed transferability depends heavily on customer and recruiter retention after the founder exits.

q.rationale: A meaningful portion of agency pricing can remain linked to a successful hire, project, or agreed placement fee rather than to disclosed labor hours, allowing some AI-enabled productivity to be retained through recruiter capacity, faster response, or higher fill throughput. Capture is incomplete because employer procurement can demand lower fees, recruiters may reinvest time in candidate and client service, placement volume is demand-constrained, and job boards, applicant-tracking vendors, internal recruiting teams, and rival agencies can pass efficiency gains to customers.
q.change_evidence: Contract-level evidence on fee basis, repricing frequency, fill rates, recruiter desk capacity, contribution margin, and client retention before and after AI adoption would materially change the estimate.
caveats: Public evidence does not quantify the 561311 revenue mix by contingency, fixed, retained-project, or time-based fees.
Saved recruiter time does not create value if client demand or candidate supply is the binding constraint.
Competitive pass-through may vary sharply by specialty and local labor market.

d5.bridge: BLS projects constant-dollar output for the broader Employment Services industry, NAICS 561300, from $424.6 billion in 2024 to $545.2 billion in 2034. Taking the square root of the ten-year output ratio gives an approximately 1.13 five-year demand multiplier. The parent industry includes temporary help, executive search, and PEO activity and is dominated by models outside 561311, so it can overstate placement-agency demand if temporary staffing expands faster, or understate it if permanent placement takes share. BLS's 6% projected 2024-2034 growth for Human Resources Specialists is used only as a directional cross-check because it includes in-house HR demand.
d5.rationale: Employers continue to need sourcing, screening, matching, and hiring support, and BLS projects real output growth in employment services plus faster-than-average growth in the related HR specialist occupation. Demand can still be cyclical and vulnerable to hiring slowdowns, client insourcing, direct sourcing platforms, and AI-enabled self-service. The range therefore allows both modest contraction and strong expansion around the broad-industry baseline.
d5.change_evidence: A 561311-specific real revenue forecast segmented by occupation specialty, client size, permanent-placement volumes, fee rates, and internal-versus-outsourced recruiting share would materially move the estimate.
caveats: The principal projection is for 561300 rather than 561311.
Real industry output is not a direct measure of constant-quality demand for a typical target agency.
Recruiting demand is highly cyclical and sensitive to hiring volumes and vacancy duration.

o.rationale: An operator remains important for client development, intake calibration, candidate trust, nuanced screening, persuasion, negotiation, exceptions, compliance, and accountability. LinkedIn reports rising emphasis on relationship development and quality of hire while confidence in measuring quality remains limited, consistent with a human-led service model. Operator need is lower for standardized, high-volume placements where employers and candidates can self-serve through software and AI agents, but higher in specialized or relationship-intensive niches.
o.change_evidence: Evidence on autonomous recruiting-platform fill rates, client willingness to purchase software instead of service, human escalation frequency, candidate conversion, quality-of-hire outcomes, and retention by specialty would materially change the estimate.
caveats: The estimate separates continued need for an accountable operator from the number of staff required.
Human involvement may persist because of regulation or client preference even if technical capability advances faster.
Standardized roles and proprietary candidate networks can produce very different substitution risk.

narrative.executive_view: Employment placement agencies combine an unusually digital recruiting workflow with a relationship- and judgment-heavy client service. AI can assist a substantial portion of search, matching, drafting, outreach, scheduling, recordkeeping, and first-pass screening, but reliable placement still depends on human calibration, persuasion, exceptions, and accountability. The attractive operating form is a repeat-client agency whose relationships, recruiter team, process, and data survive the founder; a personal recruiter practice is materially less transferable.
narrative.ai_impact: Near-term impact is more likely to be recruiter augmentation than fully autonomous placement. Current recruiting-professional evidence shows active experimentation and meaningful reported time savings, while the task map shows both machine-suitable digital work and frequent high-contact decisions. Over five years, integrated tools can increase desk capacity and response speed, but privacy, bias, adverse-impact testing, data quality, candidate experience, and client-specific judgment should keep humans in the loop for consequential selection steps.
narrative.value_capture: Outcome- or project-linked fees can let an agency retain some productivity through more searches per recruiter, faster shortlists, or better service without repricing every labor hour. Capture will vary with demand and competition: a constrained hiring market may leave capacity unused, while sophisticated clients, internal recruiting teams, job platforms, and rival agencies may claim savings through lower fees or higher service expectations.
narrative.firm_availability: The frozen firm-count estimate indicates a finite but meaningful lower-middle-market population. Actual five-year acquisition availability is narrower because founder timing, customer concentration, recruiter dependence, cyclical earnings, and relationship portability determine whether a nominal agency is a transferable control opportunity. Broad service-business transaction data demonstrate a functioning market but do not directly measure qualifying 561311 deals.
narrative.demand_durability: BLS projects real output growth for the broader Employment Services industry and faster-than-average employment growth for Human Resources Specialists, supporting continued demand for hiring expertise. Permanent-placement agencies remain exposed to hiring cycles, client insourcing, self-service sourcing, and fee pressure, so specialty diversity, repeat-client behavior, and placement outcomes matter more than the broad projection alone.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The largest uncertainties are the absence of 561311-specific workflow and transaction datasets, the broad scope of adoption surveys and industry projections, heterogeneous fee models, cyclicality, and the pace at which employers accept autonomous screening or matching. Employment-law accountability and candidate privacy can slow realization, while platform advances could accelerate substitution in standardized roles. Founder and top-recruiter concentration can also erase otherwise attractive technology economics.
narrative.principal_driver: A large digitally mediated workflow, combined with repeat employer demand and fee structures that can reward successful placement rather than hours worked, creates a credible path from AI assistance to higher recruiter capacity and retained margin.
narrative.principal_weakness: Value is vulnerable to hiring cyclicality and to the possibility that client and candidate relationships reside with individual recruiters, while self-service platforms and internal teams can compete away productivity gains.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS Manual: 561311 Employment Placement Agencies
sources.supports: Defines 561311 as vacancy listing and applicant recruiting, referral, or placement where placed individuals are not agency employees; identifies included examples and cross-industry exclusions.

sources.title: O*NET OnLine: Human Resources Specialists (13-1071.00)
sources.supports: Provides the recruiter-adjacent task map and work context, including application review and matching, interviewing, candidate search, recommendations, communications, records, regulatory knowledge, and frequent interpersonal contact.

sources.title: LinkedIn: The Future of Recruiting 2025
sources.supports: Reports methodology and results from 1,271 recruiting professionals: 37% experimenting with or integrating generative AI, about 20% average workload reduction among users, increasing importance of relationship development and quality of hire, and limited confidence in quality measurement.

sources.title: U.S. EEOC: What is the EEOC's Role in AI?
sources.supports: States that federal employment discrimination laws apply to AI and other technologies used in recruiting, screening, and hiring, supporting implementation-friction and human-accountability considerations.

sources.title: U.S. BLS: Industry Employment and Output Projections, 2024-2034
sources.supports: Projects Employment Services (561300) real output from $424.6 billion in 2024 to $545.2 billion in 2034 and employment from 3.332 million to 3.240 million, providing the broad-industry demand proxy.

sources.title: U.S. BLS Occupational Outlook Handbook: Human Resources Specialists
sources.supports: Projects 6% employment growth for Human Resources Specialists from 2024 to 2034 and about 81,800 annual openings, used as a directional demand cross-check.

sources.title: BizBuySell 2025 Fourth Quarter Insight Report
sources.supports: Reports 2025 U.S. small-business transaction activity and service-sector deal statistics, including a 4% volume increase, $340,000 median sale price, and 2.52 median cash-flow multiple, used as a broad transfer-market proxy.
